proximetricsR 0.6.6
  • Reference
    • An introduction to the proximetricsR package
    • Spectral pre-processing recipes
    • Mathematical overview of regression algorithms
    • ProxiMate: Building applications
    • ProxiScout: Building applications
    • ProxiMate: Structure of the applications
    • ProxiScout: Structure of the applications
    • ProxiMate: Read and recalibrate applications

Mathematical overview of regression algorithms

Claudio Orellano and Leonardo Ramirez-Lopez

2026-07-18

Source: vignettes/ac-mathematical-overview-of-regression-algorithms.qmd

1 Introduction

This package implements three main regression approaches: i) the classical partial least squares regression (PLSR) algorithm (Wold 1975), ii) the modified PLSR algorithm used in near-infrared spectroscopy (Shenk and Westerhaus 1991; Westerhaus 2014), and iii) a proprietary PLSR extension called XLS. The package also provides an NIRWise PLUS-compatible implementation, referred to as nwp, which follows the modified PLSR structure but applies an additional slope correction to the weights and scores.

2 Correlation vs. covariance

Before jumping into the description of the regression algorithms used in this R library, we first take a quick look at the definition and calculation of both the sample covariance and correlation. Note that throughout this document, we indicate an overwritten variable value by an arrow to the left, to emphasize that a new value has been assigned.

Assume we are given a spectral data matrix X=[x1,…,xM]X = [x_1, \dots, x_M], where each xm=[xm1,…,xmN]Tx_m = [x_{m1}, \dots, x_{mN}]^T is a column vector of length NN. Additionally, we have a matrix of targets Y=[y1,…,yN]TY = [y_1,\dots,y_N]^T, which indicates the reference values of one single property. While it is possible to adjust the PLS algorithms to allow regressing on several properties at once, it is often the case that individual models for each property performs better.

Note that it is crucial for the PLS algorithm that both spectra and targets are centered by their means, meaning that the mean of each column vector is equal to zero:

μxm:=1N∑j=1Nxjm=0 \mu_{x_m} := \frac{1}{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} x_{jm} = 0

for every m=1,…,M.m=1,\dots,M. If a column vector of either the spectra or the targets are non-centered, we may simply center them as follows:

xm←xm−μxm,Y←Y−μY. \begin{split} x_m &\leftarrow x_m - \mu_{x_m},\ Y &\leftarrow Y - \mu_Y. \end{split}

Hereafter, we assume that both target vector YY and spectral matrix XX are mean-centered without loss of generality. This allows us to simplify the calculations of the involved statistical terminology, as we shall explore in the rest of this section.

The sample covariance of the centered matrices XX and YY can be calculated as follows:

cov⁡(X,Y)=(cov⁡(xm),Y)m=1M=(1N−1∑j=1N((xjm−μxm)(yj−μY)))m=1M=(1N−1∑j=1Nxjmyj)m=1M=(1N−1xmTY)m=1M=1N−1XTY. \begin{split} \operatorname{cov}(X,Y) &= (\operatorname{cov}(x_m), Y)_{m=1}^M\\ &= (\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{j=1}^N ((x_{jm}-\mu_{x_m})(y_j-\mu_Y)))_{m=1}^M\\ &= (\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{j=1}^N x_{jm}y_j)_{m=1}^M\\ &= (\frac{1}{N-1} x_m^T Y)_{m=1}^M\\ &= \frac{1}{N-1} X^T Y. \end{split}

On the other hand, if we consider YY as a vector, and split the matrix XX into column vectors xmx_m, we may calculate the sample Pearson correlation coefficient of xmx_m and YY as follows:

cor⁡(xm,Y):=cov(xm,Y)σxmσY=xmTY(∑j=1Nxjm2)(∑j=1Nyj2). \begin{split} \operatorname{cor}(x_m,Y) &:= \frac{cov(x_m,Y)}{\sigma_{x_m} \sigma_Y} \\ &= \frac{x_m^TY}{\sqrt{(\sum_{j=1}^{N} x_{jm}^2)(\sum_{j=1}^{N} y_j^2)}}. \end{split}

where the sample standard deviation is defined as follows:

σxm:=1N−1∑j=1N(xjm−μxm)2=1N−1∑j=1Nxjm2, \begin{split} \sigma_{x_m} &:= \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{j=1}^N (x_{jm}-\mu_{x_m})^2}\\ &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{j=1}^N x_{jm}^2}, \end{split}

Similarly, the Pearson correlation matrix of the full spectral matrix XX and the targets YY is defined as:

Cor⁡(X,Y)=[cor⁡(xm,Y)]m=1M, \begin{split} \operatorname{Cor}(X,Y) = [\operatorname{cor}(x_m, Y)]_{m=1}^M, \end{split}

such that Cor⁡(X,Y)∈ℝM×1\operatorname{Cor}(X,Y) \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times 1}.

Note that this does not imply that there is a linear relationship between the sample correlation and covariance, as each column vector xmx_m has a different standard deviation σxm\sigma_{x_m} in general.

3 Partial Least Squares Regression algorithms

The goal of this section is to provide insight into how the PLSR algorithms are implemented in the package. The different variants share the same broad score-loading-deflation structure, but they differ in the way the weight vector is computed and, in the case of the NIRWise PLUS-compatible implementation, in the additional slope correction applied to weights and scores.

A common PLSR-type implementation may be summarized as follows:

  • First, we calculate weights. In standard PLSR, these weights are based on covariances between the observations and targets. In modified PLSR, they are based on Pearson correlations. These weights indicate how much each individual wavelength contributes to the component being extracted.

  • Then, we project the observed spectra onto the space of weights to obtain the scores. In other words, we compress the spectral data information into a lower-dimensional space, where each wavelength has influence on the outcome according to the weights.

  • Afterwards, the loadings are computed by regressing the columns of the observations on the scores. In particular, regression is done on the scores of the spectra, and not on spectral matrix itself. As such, the scores are the central aspect of the algorithm.

  • Lastly, the scores and loadings are used to deflate the observations. More precisely, we remove the aspects of the spectrum which we already have explained via the information of the scores.

The procedure is repeated for a pre-determined number of factors using the deflated spectra for each repetition.

This document is organized as follows. In Standard PLSR algorithm, we describe the classical covariance-based PLSR algorithm following the NIPALS approach (Wold 1975). In Modified PLSR algorithm, we describe the modified PLSR algorithm used in near-infrared spectroscopy (Shenk and Westerhaus 1991; Westerhaus 2014). In NIRWise PLUS-compatible PLSR implementation, we describe the implementation used to reproduce the NIRWise PLUS behaviour. In Derived matrices and diagnostics, we show how additional matrices may be obtained, which serve as useful analysis tools to check the performance of the algorithm. In PLS predictions, we discuss two separate, but mathematically related, ways of calculating predictions for new data. Finally, in Extended PLSR: the XLS algorithm, we describe the proprietary XLS weighting scheme.

4 Standard PLSR algorithm

Classical partial least squares regression can be described using the NIPALS approach (Wold 1975). In the standard PLS1 formulation, the weights are selected according to the covariance between the deflated spectral matrix and the response.

Consider a given spectral matrix XX with NN observations, each containing MM wavelengths, such that X∈ℝN×MX \in \mathbb{R}^{N\times M}:

X=[x1,…,xN]T=[x1,…,xM]=(xij)i=1,…,Nj=1,…,M. X = [x_1, \dots, x_N]^T = [x_1, \dots, x_M] = (x_{ij})_{i=1,\dots,N}^{j=1,\dots,M}.

Similarly, we have corresponding targets or reference values Y∈ℝN×1Y \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1} for each observation. Note that we consider only one vector of targets at a time. If there are more reference vectors to be considered, one computes PLSR on each of them separately. As discussed before, we assume that the observations XX and targets YY are mean-centered.

The following calculations are repeated FF times. We denote by ii the current factor, running through i=1,…,Fi=1,\dots, F, and by X(i)X^{(i)} the corresponding observations for factor ii, starting with the mean-centered XX:

X(1)=X. X^{(1)} = X.

For every factor, X(i)X^{(i)} will be adjusted; as such, we use this notation to emphasize which matrix is utilized.

In standard PLSR, we begin the procedure by calculating the weights of the current component ii from the covariance between X(i)X^{(i)} and YY:

w(i)=cov⁡(X(i),Y)=1N−1(X(i))TY∈ℝM×1. w^{(i)} = \operatorname{cov}(X^{(i)},Y) = \frac{1}{N-1}(X^{(i)})^T Y \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times 1}.

These weights w(i)w^{(i)} describe how much each column of X(i)X^{(i)} covaries with the reference values YY.

We project the observations X(i)X^{(i)} onto a lower-dimensional space to obtain the scores as follows:

t(i)=X(i)w(i). t^{(i)} = X^{(i)}w^{(i)}.

In particular, t(i)∈ℝN×1t^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1}. The loadings p(i)∈ℝM×1p^{(i)}\in \mathbb{R}^{M \times 1} are obtained by regressing the columns of X(i)X^{(i)} on the scores:

p(i)=(X(i))Tt(i)|t(i)|22. p^{(i)} = \frac{(X^{(i)})^T t^{(i)}}{| t^{(i)}|^2_2}.

The response loading is:

q(i)=YTt(i)|t(i)|22. q^{(i)} = \frac{Y^T t^{(i)}}{| t^{(i)} |^2_2}.

As a last step of the PLSR algorithm, we deflate our observations by removing the present component from X(i)X^{(i)} as follows:

X(i+1)=X(i)−t(i)p(i)T. X^{(i+1)} = X^{(i)} - t^{(i)} p^{(i)T}.

Now, we may repeat the algorithm for the next factor i+1i+1 by using X(i+1)X^{(i+1)} in place of X(i)X^{(i)} for the calculation of the PLSR weights at the beginning of the procedure until we have reached the last factor FF.

5 Modified PLSR algorithm

Modified PLSR is a variant widely used in near-infrared spectroscopy calibration. It is commonly associated with the work of Shenk and Westerhaus (Shenk and Westerhaus 1991). Westerhaus later described the motivation for this modification as replacing raw covariance-based wavelength weights with a normalized association between each wavelength and the response (Westerhaus 2014).

In the implementation described here, this normalized association is expressed as the Pearson correlation coefficient. The algorithm follows the same general score-loading-deflation structure as standard PLSR, but replaces the covariance-based weights by correlation-based weights.

For every factor ii, starting with X(1)=XX^{(1)} = X, the weights are computed as:

w(i)=Cor⁡(X(i),Y)∈ℝM×1. w^{(i)} = \operatorname{Cor}(X^{(i)},Y) \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times 1}.

These weights w(i)w^{(i)} show how much each column of X(i)X^{(i)} correlates with the reference values YY.

The observations X(i)X^{(i)} are then projected onto the space of weights to obtain the scores:

t(i)=X(i)w(i). t^{(i)} = X^{(i)}w^{(i)}.

The loadings are obtained by regressing the columns of X(i)X^{(i)} on the scores:

p(i)=(X(i))Tt(i)|t(i)|22. p^{(i)} = \frac{(X^{(i)})^T t^{(i)}}{| t^{(i)}|^2_2}.

The response loading is:

q(i)=YTt(i)|t(i)|22. q^{(i)} = \frac{Y^T t^{(i)}}{| t^{(i)} |^2_2}.

Finally, the observations are deflated:

X(i+1)=X(i)−t(i)p(i)T. X^{(i+1)} = X^{(i)} - t^{(i)} p^{(i)T}.

Thus, the main difference between standard and modified PLSR is the construction of the weight vector: standard PLSR uses covariance-based weights, whereas modified PLSR uses correlation-based weights.

6 NIRWise PLUS-compatible PLSR implementation

The NIRWise PLUS-compatible implementation in proximetricsR, referred to as type = "nwp", follows the modified PLSR structure by using correlation-based weights, but applies an additional slope correction to the weights and scores. This section preserves the details of that implementation.

Consider a given spectral matrix XX with NN observations, each containing MM wavelengths, such that X∈ℝN×MX \in \mathbb{R}^{N\times M}:

X=[x1,…,xN]T=[x1,…,xM]=(xij)i=1,…,Nj=1,…,M. X = [x_1, \dots, x_N]^T = [x_1, \dots, x_M] = (x_{ij})_{i=1,\dots,N}^{j=1,\dots,M}.

Similarly, we have corresponding targets or reference values Y∈ℝN×1Y \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1} for each observation. Note that we consider only one vector of targets at a time. If there are more reference vectors to be considered, one computes PLSR on each of them separately. As discussed before, we assume that the observations XX and targets YY are mean-centered.

The following calculations are repeated FF times (FF is pre-determined, often chosen as 1515). We denote by ii the current factor, running through i=1,…,Fi=1,\dots, F, and by X(i)X^{(i)} the corresponding observations for factors ii, starting with the mean-centered XX:

X(1)=X. X^{(1)} = X.

For every factor, X(i)X^{(i)} will be adjusted; as such, we use this notation to emphasize which matrix is utilized.

We begin the procedure by calculating the weights of the current component ii as the Pearson correlation coefficient matrix of X(i)X^{(i)} and YY:

w(i)=Cor⁡(X(i),Y)∈ℝM×1. w^{(i)} = \operatorname{Cor}(X^{(i)},Y) \in \mathbb{R}^{M \times 1}.

These weights w(i)w^{(i)} show by how much each column of X(i)X^{(i)} correlates to the references values YY. In the standard PLSR formulation, following the NIPALS approach (Wold 1975), the weights are instead selected according to the covariance between the spectral matrix X(i)X^{(i)} and the reference values YY.

We project the observations X(i)X^{(i)} onto a lower-dimensional space to obtain the scores as follows:

t(i)=X(i)w(i). t^{(i)} = X^{(i)}w^{(i)}.

In particular, t(i)∈ℝN×1t^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1}. Common PLS algorithms now usually continue by normalization of the scores or by calculating the loadings. However, in the NIRWise PLUS-compatible implementation, we first compute a regression of YY on the scores:

a(i)=YTt(i)|t(i)|22∈ℝ, a^{(i)} = \frac{Y^T t^{(i)}}{| t^{(i)}|^2_2} \in \mathbb{R},

and then adjust both weights and scores according to this factor:

w(i)←a(i)w(i),t(i)←a(i)t(i). \begin{split} w^{(i)} &\leftarrow a^{(i)} w^{(i)},\ t^{(i)} &\leftarrow a^{(i)} t^{(i)}. \end{split}

The reason for this adjustment factor a(i)a^{(i)} is that we may later simply add all calculated scores to obtain the estimates. Additionally, we can directly compute the bias of our estimation in component ii as follows:

bias⁡(i)=μY−μt(i), \operatorname{bias}^{(i)} = \mu_{Y}-\mu_{t^{(i)}},

which allows us to further adjust the scores to an unbiased estimation of YY:

t(i)←t(i)−bias⁡(i). t^{(i)} \leftarrow t^{(i)} - \operatorname{bias}^{(i)}.

Note that because the scores are regressed on the centered YY, this bias should be very close to zero. However, due to computer precision, some small values are observable.

By regressing the columns of X(i)X^{(i)} on the scores t(i)t^{(i)}, we obtain the loadings p(i)∈ℝM×1p^{(i)}\in \mathbb{R}^{M \times 1} as follows:

p(i)=(X(i))Tt(i)|t(i)|22. p^{(i)} = \frac{(X^{(i)})^T t^{(i)}}{| t^{(i)}|^2_2}.

As a last step of the PLSR algorithm, we deflate our observations by removing the present component from X(i)X^{(i)} as follows:

X(i+1)=X(i)−t(i)p(i)T. X^{(i+1)} = X^{(i)} - t^{(i)} p^{(i)T}.

Now, we may repeat the algorithm for the next factor i+1i+1 by using X(i+1)X^{(i+1)} in place of X(i)X^{(i)} for the calculation of the PLSR weights at the beginning of the procedure until we have reached the last factor FF.

7 Derived matrices and diagnostics

In this section, we provide insights on how common additional matrices for improved analysis of the procedure are obtained. As before, we assume that the spectra XX and the target YY are mean-centered. However, for some of the calculations below, we also require the actual reference values. As such, we denote by Y(0)Y^{(0)} the raw or original target vector.

To obtain the scaled scores, we first compute the scale, which is given by the standard deviation of t(i)t^{(i)}:

scale⁡(i)=σt(i). \operatorname{scale}^{(i)} = \sigma_{t^{(i)}}.

This allows us to normalize t(i)t^{(i)} and to obtain the scores scaled of component ii:

t(i)**=t(i)/σ*t(i). t^{(i)}** = t^{(i)} / \sigma*{t^{(i)}}.

The Mahalanobis distance of the scaled scores may be calculated as follows:

D(i)=∑n=1i(t*(n))2i. D^{(i)} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{n=1}^{i}\big(t^{(n)}_*\big)^2}{i}}.

In particular, we calculate the Euclidean distance of the sum of all previous components of the scaled scores.

For the estimates in the NIRWise PLUS-compatible implementation, note that we have already regressed YY on the scores t(i)t^{(i)}. Hence, they may be calculated directly by summing all obtained scores. However, the regression was done on the centered YY. Therefore, we have to add the mean of the original, non-centered Y(0)Y^{(0)} back to obtain the estimates Ŷ(i)\hat{Y}^{(i)} as follows:

Ŷ(i)=μY(0)+∑n=1it(n). \hat{Y}^{(i)} = \mu_{Y^{(0)}} + \sum_{n=1}^{i} t^{(n)}.

The residuals r(i)r^{(i)} are defined as the difference between the targets YY and the estimates. Again, since estimation is done on non-centered targets, we must use the raw reference vector:

r(i)=Y(0)−Ŷ(i). r^{(i)} = Y^{(0)} - \hat{Y}^{(i)}.

We may also calculate the regression coefficients β(i)\beta^{(i)} as follows.

First, we calculate the projection matrix AA, which projects the observations XX onto the space of scores:

A=(WPT)−1W∈ℝF×M, A = (W P^T)^{-1} W \in \mathbb{R}^{F \times M},

where WW denotes the matrix of all weights and PP the matrix of all loadings. In particular, the following holds true:

t(i)=AiXT, t^{(i)} = A_iX^T,

where AiA_i denotes the iith row of AA. Using the calculation of the estimates, we may alternatively compute the estimates as follows:

Ŷ(i)=μY(0)+∑n=1it(n)=μY(0)+(∑n=1iAn)XT=μY(0)+β(i)XT, \begin{split} \hat{Y}^{(i)} &= \mu_{Y^{(0)}} + \sum_{n=1}^{i} t^{(n)}\\ &= \mu_{Y^{(0)}} + (\sum_{n=1}^i A_n)X^{T}\\ &= \mu_{Y^{(0)}} + \beta^{(i)} X^T, \end{split}

where we defined the regression coefficients

β(i)=∑n=1iAn. \beta^{(i)} = \sum_{n=1}^i A_n.

Therefore, the calculation of the regression coefficients simplifies the computation of estimates. As we shall see, they also allow for a quick and simple calculation for the predictions (see PLS predictions). Furthermore, they allow an interpretation of how each column of XX contributes to the estimates and predictions of YY.

The residuum R(i)∈ℝN×1R^{(i)} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times 1} is calculated by summing the squares of each row of our deflated X(i+1)X^{(i+1)}:

R(i)=∑m=1M(xm(i+1))2, R^{(i)} = \sum_{m=1}^{M} \big(x^{(i+1)}_{m} \big)^2,

where multiplication is meant element-wise.

Furthermore, we note that this package does not provide any YY-loadings for the NIRWise PLUS-compatible implementation, which are commonly supplied in PLSR procedures. However, as we shall see, they do not provide any contribution to the analysis of this implementation. First of all, the definition of YY-loadings is as follows:

q(i)=YTt(i)|t(i)|22. q^{(i)} = \frac{Y^T t^{(i)}}{| t^{(i)} |^2_2}.

Note that this is similar to the calculation of factor a(i)a^{(i)} in NIRWise PLUS-compatible PLSR implementation. In particular, we are regressing YY on the scores again; but since we have already adjusted the scores according to the regression factor a(i)a^{(i)}, we should get a constant YY-loading of 11. Indeed, if we denote by t̃(i)\tilde{t}^{(i)} the score before the regression, i.e.

t̃(i)=t(i)a(i), \tilde{t}^{(i)} = \frac{t^{(i)}}{a^{(i)}},

then we obtain

q(i)=YTt(i)|t(i)|22=YTa(i)t̃(i)(a(i))2|t̃(i)|22=YTt̃(i)a(i)t̃(i)Tt̃(i)=a(i)a(i)=1. \begin{split} q^{(i)} &= \frac{Y^T t^{(i)}}{| t^{(i)} |^2_2}\\ &= \frac{Y^T a^{(i)}\tilde{t}^{(i)}}{(a^{(i)})^2| \tilde{t}^{(i)} |^2_2}\\ &= \frac{Y^T \tilde{t}^{(i)}}{a^{(i)}\tilde{t}^{(i)T} \tilde{t}^{(i)}}\\ &= \frac{a^{(i)}}{a^{(i)}}\\ &= 1. \end{split}

As such, outputting the YY-loadings is not of any actual use for the NIRWise PLUS-compatible implementation and therefore omitted.

8 PLS predictions

In this section, we illustrate how the matrices of the previous section may be used to obtain predictions. We assume that the PLSR algorithm has already been fully calculated and all matrices of the previous section are given. We indicate newly calculated vectors and matrices by a tilde, while the given ones use the same notation as in the previous section. Furthermore, we note that the predictions in case of XLS (discussed in Extended PLSR: the XLS algorithm) are done similarly with the respective matrices. Hence, there is no need to discuss the prediction for XLS separately.

We provide two different approaches for the predictions: one uses the scores, while the other one makes use of the regression coefficients. Of course, both approaches provide equivalent results, but the necessary matrices differ.

8.1 Predictions using scores

For this approach, we must be provided with the following matrices:

  • Mean of the raw spectra before centering: μX(0)\mu_{X^{(0)}}.

  • Mean of the raw targets before centering: μY(0)\mu_{Y^{(0)}}

  • Weights of every component: w(i)w^{(i)}.

  • Biases of every component: bias⁡(i)\operatorname{bias}^{(i)}.

  • Loadings of each component: p(i)p^{(i)}

Assume we are given Ñ\tilde{N} new observations in form of X̃∈ℝÑ×M\tilde{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{\tilde{N} \times M}, where we assume them to have a similar number of wavelengths as the given spectra X(0)X^{(0)}.

We start by centering the columns of our new observations by the means of our calibration:

X̃(1)=X̃−μX(0). \tilde{X}^{(1)} = \tilde{X} - \mu_{X^{(0)}}.

We calculate the predictions based on the number of factors included, i.e. we again run through i=1,…,Fi=1, \dots, F.

As a first step, we project X̃(i)\tilde{X}^{(i)} onto the space of the calibrated scores using the given weights:

t̃(i)=X̃(i)w(i). \tilde{t}^{(i)} = \tilde{X}^{(i)} w^{(i)}.

We then adjust the obtained scores according to the previously obtained bias:

t̃(i)←t̃(i)−bias⁡(i). \tilde{t}^{(i)} \leftarrow \tilde{t}^{(i)} - \operatorname{bias}^{(i)}.

The prediction can now be obtained by summing all previous scores and adding the mean of Y(0)Y^{(0)} as follows:

Ŷ̃(i)=μY(0)+∑n=1it̃(n). \tilde{\hat{Y}}^{(i)} = \mu_{Y^{(0)}} + \sum_{n=1}^{i} \tilde{t}^{(n)}.

We then deflate X̃(i)\tilde{X}^{(i)} using the new scores and the loadings of the calibration:

X̃(i+1)=X̃(i)−t̃(i)p(i)T, \tilde{X}^{(i+1)} = \tilde{X}^{(i)} - \tilde{t}^{(i)} p^{(i)T},

and restart the procedure using the deflated X̃(i+1)\tilde{X}^{(i+1)}.

8.2 Predictions using regression coefficients

For this approach, we must be provided with the following matrices:

  • Mean of the raw spectra before centering: μX(0)\mu_{X^{(0)}}.

  • Mean of the raw targets before centering: μY(0)\mu_{Y^{(0)}}

  • Correct regression coefficients: β̃\tilde{\beta}.

Again, we assume that Ñ\tilde{N} new observations in form of X̃∈ℝÑ×M\tilde{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{\tilde{N} \times M} are given. We center the columns of our new observations by the means of our calibration:

X̃←X̃−μX(0). \tilde{X} \leftarrow \tilde{X} - \mu_{X^{(0)}}.

The predictions now may be obtained immediately for every component ii by computing

Ŷ̃=β̃X̃T+μY(0), \tilde{\hat{Y}} = \tilde{\beta} \tilde{X}^T + \mu_{Y^{(0)}},

where each row of Ŷ̃∈ℝF×Ñ\tilde{\hat{Y}} \in \mathbb{R}^{F \times \tilde{N}} provides the prediction based on the corresponding number of components.

9 Extended PLSR: the XLS algorithm

In this section, we discuss how the weights for the XLS algorithm are calculated. XLS is a proprietary extension of the PLSR framework used in BUCHI workflows. Note that the calculation of the weights is the only difference to the PLSR algorithms described above; the rest of the outputs can be obtained in a similar way as in the previous sections, including predictions.

For ease of notation, we drop the mention of the current component; in particular, XX corresponds to X(i)X^{(i)} in the preceding PLSR algorithms.

As before, we assume that the observations XX and the targets YY are centered. To calculate the weights, we run through all columns of XX and calculate the weights element-wise. In particular, the following calculation is done for all m=1,…,Mm=1,\dots,M.

Let us consider some fixed m∈[1,M]m \in [1,M]. We define the following interval

Jm+=[m+mmin,min⁡(M,m+mmax)], J_m^+ = [m+m_{\min}, \min(M,m+m_{\max})],

where the default choice is mmin=3,mmax=15m_\text{min}=3, m_\text{max}=15. We proceed by computing the differences of column mm of XX with each column in interval Jm+J_m^+:

Xmjd=xm−xj,∀j∈Jm+. X_{mj}^d = x_m - x_j, \forall j \in J_m^+.

Thereafter, we may compute the Pearson correlation coefficients between those differences and YY as follows:

dmj=cor⁡(Xmjd,Y),∀j∈Jm+. d_{mj} = \operatorname{cor}(X_{mj}^d, Y), \forall j \in J_m^+.

The weights vector can now be obtained as follows:

wm=wm+dmj,∀j∈Jm+, w_m = w_m + d_{mj}, \forall j \in J_m^+,

wj=wj−dmj,∀j∈Jm+, w_j = w_j - d_{mj}, \forall j \in J_m^+,

where wm=0w_m = 0 whenever it has not been defined yet. The resulting vector w=[w1,…,wM]w = [w_1, \dots, w_M] replaces the computation of the PLSR weights above for each component ii.

As there is no easy interpretation to the calculation of these weights, we transform above calculations to obtain an equivalent algorithm, which is hopefully easier to interpret.

Note that in the first equation above, we add all Pearson correlation coefficients of the derivatives of columns mm and all j∈Jm+j \in J_m^+ to weight mm; whereas in the second equation, we subtract the same correlations from weights wj,j∈Jm+w_j, j \in J_m^+. A careful analysis on which differences dd are subtracted from a fixed weight element mm shows that it may also be expressed in the following way:

wm=∑j∈Jm+dmj−∑j=max⁡(0,m−mmax)m−3djm. w_m = \sum_{j\in J_m^+} d_{mj} - \sum_{j = \max(0,m-m_\text{max})}^{m-3} d_{jm}.

Note that the order of jj and mm in the subscript of dd for the second sum is inverted. In particular, if we define the ‘mirrored’ interval

Jm−=[max⁡(0,m−mmax),m−mmin], J_m^- = [\max (0,m-m_\text{max}), m-m_\text{min}],

then each individual weight may be expressed as follows:

wm=∑j∈Jm+dmj−∑j∈Jm−djm. w_m = \sum_{j\in J_m^+} d_{mj} - \sum_{j\in J_m^-} d_{jm}.

A graphical representation of the intervals in relation to mm is given at the end of this section.

Note that the Pearson correlation coefficient satisfies

cor⁡(xj−xm,Y)=−cor⁡(xm−xj,Y), \operatorname{cor}(x_j - x_m,Y) = - \operatorname{cor}(x_m - x_j,Y),

and therefore, plugging in the definition of dd, we obtain

wm=∑j∈Jm+dmj−∑j∈Jm−djm=∑j∈Jm+cor⁡(xm−xj,Y)−∑j∈Jm−cor⁡(xj−xm,Y)=∑j∈Jm+cor⁡(xm−xj,Y)+∑j∈Jm−cor⁡(xm−xj,Y)=∑j∈Jm+∪Jm−cor⁡(xm−xj,Y). \begin{split} w_m &= \sum_{j\in J_m^+} d_{mj} - \sum_{j\in J_m^-} d_{jm}\\ &= \sum_{j\in J_m^+} \operatorname{cor}(x_m - x_j, Y) - \sum_{j\in J_m^-} \operatorname{cor}(x_j-x_m, Y)\\ &=\sum_{j \in J_m^+} \operatorname{cor}(x_m - x_j, Y) + \sum_{j\in J_m^-} \operatorname{cor}(x_m-x_j, Y)\\ &= \sum_{j \in J_m^+ \cup J_m^-} \operatorname{cor}(x_m - x_j, Y). \end{split}

Hence, the calculation of the XLS weights may be interpreted as follows. As illustrated in Figure 1, for each position mm we consider the neighbouring intervals Jm−J_m^- and Jm+J_m^+, truncated where necessary so that they remain within [1,M][1, M]. We then compute the first derivative of the current column mm with respect to each column in the interval J=Jm+∪Jm−J = J_m^+ \cup J_m^-. The weight at position mm is obtained as the sum of the correlations between each derivative and the target. This procedure is repeated for every m∈[1,M]m \in [1, M]. Note that for some mm, one of the intervals may be truncated or even empty (for example, when m=1m = 1, J1−J_1^- is empty). By convention, the sum over an empty interval is taken to be 00.

References

Shenk, JS, and MO Westerhaus. 1991. “Population Definition, Sample Selection, and Calibration Procedures for Near Infrared Reflectance Spectroscopy.” Crop Science 31 (2): 469–74.
Westerhaus, Mark. 2014. “Eastern Analytical Symposium Award for Outstanding Achievements in Near Infrared Spectroscopy: My Contributions to Near Infrared Spectroscopy.” NIR News 25 (8): 16–20.
Wold, Herman. 1975. “Path Models with Latent Variables: The NIPALS Approach.” In Quantitative Sociology. Elsevier.

On this page
